segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a wide-angle view of an indoor athletics stadium, showcasing a red and blue track during the Men's Pole Vault Final. The large overhead scoreboard displays 'IT'S FINALS TIME!' and 'WORLD CHAMPIONSHIPS BELGRADE 22'. An announcer's voice declares, "the men's pole vault final," followed by a brief Serbian announcement, "počinjemo takmičenjem." The English announcer continues, "The action continues tonight from one world record holder, potentially to another. It's time for the Men's Pole Vault Final." The scene dramatically transitions to a darkened stadium, illuminated by vibrant purple and blue stage lighting and dynamic effects. Large vertical screens display animated silhouettes of pole vaulters, while a digital banner scrolls 'WORLD RECORD' and 'POLE VAULT FINAL' text, with the track branded by 'SEIKO' and 'ASICS' logos. As the camera focuses on the athletes in the starting lineup, including Duplantis in blue and red, Lightfoot in green, and others in yellow and purple national team jackets, an upbeat, driving electronic music track begins, accompanied by increasing crowd noise. The announcer states, "6.19 the world record," adding, "From men looking to go even higher." The on-screen graphic lists the 'STARTING LINEUP - FINAL' as the announcer introduces "13 athletes, let's meet them first of all." He then specifically names "Lillefosse of Norway among them," followed by "Curtis Marshall, Commonwealth champion from Australia," and "Torben Blech, World Student Games medalist," as these athletes are seen warming up, stretching, clapping, and raising their arms in anticipation. The atmosphere is electric, blending focused preparation with celebratory energy.

The on-screen graphic lists the 'STARTING LINEUP - FINAL' with national flags and names, and the event is identified as 'WIC BELGRADE 22' from July 15–24, 2022. The mood is electric, with a mix of focused preparation and celebratory energy as the athletes await their turn.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of the Men's Pole Vault Final starting lineup graphic, listing athletes including Duplantis, Lillefosse, Lightfoot, Marshalls, Blech, Guttormsen, Zernikel, Broeders, Lavillenie, Vylon, Collet, and Braz. An energetic, driving background music track sets the tone as athletes begin their entrance, accompanied by a male announcer introducing them. Guttormsen, in a purple jacket, raising his arms, is highlighted as a US collegiate champion indoors. Zernikel, in a blue jacket, clapping, is identified as Olex Zernikel from Germany. Ben Broeders, in a yellow jacket, clapping, is announced as a national record holder indoors and out. Valentin Lavillenie, in a blue jacket, clapping, is introduced as a former European indoor silver medalist. Thibaut Collet, in a yellow jacket, clapping, is noted as a European Under 23 bronze medalist. The announcer then refers to "five big boys ready to go toe-to-toe." Vylon, initially in a yellow jacket and later an orange hoodie, is seen clapping as the announcer identifies him as Manoah Flune, an Olympic bronze medalist from last year. Next, Thiago Braz da Silva, in a teal jacket with a Brazilian flag patch, is seen clapping as the announcer recalls his championship win on his home track of Rio in 2016. KC Lightfoot, first in a green jacket and later a blue jacket with a U.S. flag patch, is seen clapping and smiling, described as joint fourth in the Olympics last year and the third best vaulter in the world this year. Finally, Chris Neilson, in a blue jacket with a U.S. flag patch, waves and smiles, introduced as someone making a habit of joining the 6-meter club and an Olympic silver medalist from last year. The scene transitions to a wide shot of the track and field arena, showcasing the athletes lined up on the red and blue track, branded with 'SEIKO' and 'ASICS'. Dynamic lighting, large vertical screens displaying animated pole vault silhouettes, and an overhead digital scoreboard reading 'MEN'S POLE VAULT FINAL' and 'WORLD ATHLETICS INDOOR CHAMPIONSHIPS BELGRADE 22' contribute to the electric atmosphere, filled with crowd sounds and the continuous background music, as athletes display focus and excitement.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens in a darkened indoor stadium, illuminated by vibrant purple and blue lighting, with overhead screens displaying 'MEN'S POLE VAULT FINAL'. A commentator's voice sets the stage, speaking of athletes reaching "even greater heights tonight." The camera focuses on Armand Duplantis, the Swedish athlete, in a bright yellow long-sleeve top with 'SWEDEN' and sponsor logos. He stands confidently, hands on hips, then raises his right arm in a gesture of readiness, as the commentator introduces him as "the man who improved his own world record here in this very stadium 10 days ago, Mondo Duplantis, the Olympic champion, the world record holder," noting his perfect "five out of five in 2022" and that "when he vaults, it's always 10 out of 10." A wide-angle view reveals the blue and red track, branded with 'SEIKO' and 'ASICS', and the full arena, with the digital scoreboard listing competitors for the 'WORLD ATHLETICS INDOOR CHAMPIONSHIPS BELGRADE 22'. The commentator continues, calling Duplantis "the overwhelming favorite for the gold" and "the charismatic Swede." The scene then shifts to KC Lightfoot, the USA athlete, in a maroon sleeveless top with 'USA' and an American flag patch, holding a white pole vault pole. He appears focused, adjusting his grip with a determined expression, as the commentator introduces him as "KC Lightfoot," highlighting that he's "really come of age in the last couple of seasons," was the "NCAA indoor champion," and has achieved "5.95 this season" in Germany. The electric atmosphere, dynamic lighting, and large audience underscore the high stakes of the competition, all set against a backdrop of general stadium ambiance and crowd noise.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Casey Lightfoot, an athlete in a maroon sleeveless top with 'USA' and an American flag patch, running down the red and blue track with a white pole vault pole. Commentators note he's "looking to make a nice simple early start" at 5.45m, and he makes it "look very simple," sailing over "comfortably." The dynamic Stadium Lighting System casts purple and blue hues, and large vertical screens display animated silhouettes as he plants the pole, launches, and clears the bar with a smooth arc, landing safely on the blue mat. The Digital Scoreboard overhead confirms the Men's Pole Vault Final. Commentators mention that only Armand Duplantis and Thiago Braz opted to skip 5.45m, but Lightfoot cleared it "nice and easy." After his successful vault, Lightfoot walks away, appearing focused. The scene transitions to Kurtis Marschall, an athlete in a green and yellow sleeveless top with 'AUSTRALIA' across the chest, holding a pole with a pink and white grip. On-screen graphics display his stats (age 24, personal best 5.67m, season best 5.76m). Commentators introduce him as a "Commonwealth champion" who was "fourth in Birmingham four years ago," noting he'll "need to come back and reset." He performs a focused warm-up, raises his arm, runs down the branded Track and Field Track (featuring 'SEIKO' and 'ASICS' logos), plants the pole, soars over the bar, and lands on the mat, completing his attempt. The atmosphere remains intense and professional, with photographers and officials visible around the vaulting area.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of Ben Broeders, an athlete in a white and yellow sleeveless top with 'BELGIUM' and 'TDK' branding, preparing for his pole vault attempt. Commentators note "just a few problems there" and that it's "game time" for "15 of them in total." Broeders, described as "very cool and calm, national record holder indoors and out," and having placed "12th in Doha, three years ago," sprints down the red and blue track. He plants his pole and executes a clean vault over the bar, landing safely on the mat, as the commentators observe athletes "ticking off these early heights." The camera captures multiple angles, including a low-angle shot emphasizing his height and form mid-air, and a wide-angle view showing the indoor stadium's vibrant lighting and large digital screens displaying 'MEN'S POLE VAULT FINAL' and 'WORLD ATHLETICS INDOOR CHAMPIONSHIPS BELGRADE 22'. After his successful jump, Broeders walks away, visibly satisfied, as the scoreboard updates to show his height at 5.45m, tying him with Lightfoot and Guttormsen. Meanwhile, commentators mention "Mondo Duplantis" "doing up his spikes" and "KC Lightfoot" "prowling around, bouncing up and down," along with "Chris Neilson." The scene then shifts to Valentin Lavillenie, wearing a white and red sleeveless top with 'FRANCE' and 'adidas' logos, identified as the "younger brother of Renaud," who also sprints, vaults, and lands successfully, with the commentator confirming he's "safely over." Lavillenie, "still only 30," walks off the mat with a focused expression, and the scoreboard confirms his clearance at 5.45m. The environment is dynamic, with bright stadium lights, branded track surfaces, and digital displays, maintaining an intense and competitive atmosphere.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Renaud Lavillenie, an athlete in a white and red 'FRANCE' and 'adidas' top, executing a pole vault attempt. He successfully clears the bar and lands on the mat, then walks away with a focused demeanor while interacting with a coach or official. Commentary notes his past "European indoor silver" and highlights the "long established history" of French pole vault, adding that "Renaud will be watching from back home." The scoreboard shows Lavillenie tied for first at 5.45m with Lightfoot and Guttormsen. The scene transitions to Menno Vloon of the Netherlands, wearing an orange and blue 'NED' uniform, as rhythmic background music begins. A commentator introduces him as a "Men's Pole Vault Finalist" and mentions his "brilliant bronze in Tokyo." Vloon prepares for his attempt at the "opening height of 5.45," warming up with controlled movements, gripping his pole, and then sprinting down the 'ASICS' and 'SEIKO' branded track with determination. The camera follows his powerful run-up and pole plant, capturing his graceful ascent, arching over the bar. As he clears it, a commentator exclaims "Yes!" and refers to him as "Hollandia." Vloon lands safely on the mat, then walks away, giving "a couple of claps for himself." The stadium’s dynamic purple and blue lighting system enhances the high-energy atmosphere, with the digital scoreboard displaying 'WORLD ATHLETICS INDOOR CHAMPIONSHIPS BELGRADE 22'. The commentator describes Vloon as a "really powerful young man."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with an overhead shot of Vloon, an athlete in an orange and blue 'NED' uniform, executing a pole vault attempt at the World Athletics Indoor Championships in Belgrade 22. A commentator describes how he "absolutely propels himself over that" with a "brilliant angle," noting his spikes almost tapped the camera as he clears the bar and lands safely. The scene transitions to Thibaut Collet, a French athlete in a white and blue sleeveless top, who prepares for his vault. segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a wide-angle shot of the vibrant red and blue Track and Field Track, branded with 'SEIKO' and 'ASICS', within the arena of the World Athletics Indoor Championships Belgrade 22. The Stadium Lighting System casts a high-energy glow with purple and blue hues, while the overhead Digital Scoreboard displays 'MEN'S POLE VAULT | FINAL' and competitor standings. The commentator immediately highlights Duplantis's athleticism as the focus shifts to him. Duplantis, in a bright yellow sleeveless top with 'SWEDEN' across the chest, sprints with intense focus, plants his yellow pole, and executes a powerful vault, clearing the bar with precision before landing safely on the blue mat. The commentator notes it was a "very close shave with the bar" but confirms "clearance is a clearance." A replay from a low-angle perspective emphasizes the height and technique. The commentator then announces "Mondo Duplantis" has cleared 5.85m, being the "first man to attempt it, the first man to clear it," declaring "the gold is still on." He further reports that "Lillefosse of Norway" and "Lightfoot of the USA" have "bowed out at 575." Duplantis is confirmed as the "first man over at 585," leading the competition, with the commentator recalling his recent world indoor record of 6.19m less than two weeks prior. The scene transitions to Christopher Nilsen, in a red and blue sleeveless top with 'USA', who also sprints, vaults, and lands successfully, introduced by the commentator as "Chris Neilson." The Digital Scoreboard updates to show Duplantis leading at 5.75m, while other competitors are listed with lower heights. The environment remains electric with crowd noise, as the segment concludes with a close-up of Duplantis walking back, pole in hand, observed by officials and the crowd, with the commentator concluding, "So far so good for Duplantis."

On-screen text and a digital scoreboard confirm the setting as the Men's Pole Vault Final at the World Athletics Indoor Championships Belgrade 22, with Duplantis leading at 6.05m. An announcer's voice builds anticipation, asking if "the final word" will go to "Mondo Duplantis for a world record," calling him "the rockstar." Duplantis then sprints down the red and blue track, plants the pole, and executes a powerful vault, clearing the bar set at 6.20m as confirmed by the 'MONDO' digital display. Duplantis walks off the mat with a satisfied expression, approaching the stands to celebrate with fans. He hugs a blonde woman in a blue jacket, raises his arm in triumph, and interacts warmly with spectators, including a man in an orange shirt who records the moment on his phone. The atmosphere is electric, with Swedish flags waving and fans capturing the moment on their devices, as the announcer declares, "The curtain comes down on an absolutely incredible, incredible three days in Belgrade," noting that "everybody is on their feet" and "the entire stadium is now jumping." The announcer praises the "Serbian hosts" for an "amazing job," concluding that "Mondo Duplantis delivered" as if "perfectly to script." The bright lighting highlights the vibrant arena colors, and a digital timer near the vaulting area counts down from 2:07 to 2:05, capturing the emotional high of a successful vault and the athlete's connection with his supporters.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with athlete Mondo Duplantis, in a bright yellow 'SWEDEN' top, walking confidently with his yellow and purple pole vault pole, adjusting his hair, and briefly conversing with a coach or teammate in a dark blue 'BAUHAUS' shirt, shaking hands. The atmosphere is electric, with spectators waving Swedish flags and the digital scoreboard displaying 'WORLD RECORD'. A male commentator exclaims, "believe what Mondo Duplantis has just done," noting he "had to wait until the very end... to try and deliver." The camera shifts to a wide-angle view of the red and blue track, branded 'TDK' and 'WORLD ATHLETICS', with a digital timer counting down. Duplantis sprints powerfully, plants the pole, and launches into a graceful arc, clearing the bar at 6.20m, confirmed by the 'MONDO' display. The commentator declares, "This whole place is bouncing!" and notes, "You could be forgiven for thinking he's done it for Serbia, rather than Sweden," highlighting the local support amidst the constant crowd cheering and applause. Duplantis lands safely on the 'WORLD ATHLETICS INDOOR CHAMPIONSHIPS BELGRADE 22' mat, raising his arms in triumph as the 'WORLD RECORD' message flashes. The male commentator adds, "It wasn't the tidiest clearance, but it doesn't matter," and addresses a co-commentator, "Hannah England, what a way... for these three days to come to an end. The very moment we thought it was over, and we finish with a world record. That is incredible!" Hannah England then adds, "Oh, but that is poetry in motion. His first attempt didn't look that good, his second attempt looked even worse, and look at the way he soars over that world record attempt." Duplantis walks off the mat with a satisfied expression, approaches the stands to hug a blonde woman in a blue jacket, and raises his arm in victory, while a man in an orange shirt records the moment. Braz, in a green and yellow 'BRASIL' top, approaches Duplantis to shake hands respectfully, and a young fan receives an item from Braz, all under the vibrant stadium lighting.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a wide-angle, high-angle shot of the brightly lit indoor athletics stadium during the Men's Pole Vault Final at the World Indoor Championships Belgrade 22. The arena glows with vibrant purple and blue hues, illuminating the red and blue track, which is clearly branded with 'TDK' and 'WORLD ATHLETICS' on its barriers. The vaulting area is meticulously set up with the landing mat, bar, and timing devices, while a Mondo Digital Display shows 'DEPTH 0 HEIGHT 6.20', indicating the current bar height. The digital scoreboard and on-screen graphics prominently display 'MEN'S POLE VAULT | FINAL' and the current results, showing Armand Duplantis (SWE) in first place with a world record (WR) of 6.20m, followed by Thiago Braz (BRA) at 5.95m (AR) and Christopher Nilsen (USA) at 5.90m. A Starting Lineup Graphic also lists the top competitors, including Duplantis, Nilsen, and Braz. Throughout this, a male commentator exclaims with excitement, "What a finish! What a finish!" and confirms, "Mondo Duplantis breaks his own world indoor record," praising him as "fearless and flawless." He also announces, "Thiago Braz the silver, Chris Neilson the bronze," before concluding that "the applause and the history books belong to Mondo." The camera remains static, capturing the electric atmosphere as spectators fill the stands, some waving flags, including a prominent Swedish flag, reflecting the commentator's observation that "people are stunned here in Belgrade." A digital timer near the vaulting area counts down from 2:07 to 2:05. The scene transitions to a close-up of the scoreboard, emphasizing the final results, before returning to the wide shot of the stadium, which, despite no athletes actively competing in this segment, is charged with anticipation and celebration, particularly for Duplantis, who is later shown celebrating with a fist pump and hugging a woman in a blue jacket in the stands. The presence of officials, photographers, and fans further enhances the professional and festive atmosphere of the event.
